ontology.foodex2_description: Wheat flour, whole grain (wholemeal), stone-ground or roller-milled

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard single-chapati portion is approximately 30g of dry atta flour, yielding one medium-sized flatbread when cooked.

nutrient_source.title: Wholegrain Wheat Flour / Atta — HPB Singapore Foodo Database, Food Code F05040018; cross-referenced with ICMR-NIN Indian Food Composition Tables 2017
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board (HPB), Singapore; Indian Council of Medical Research — National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Foodo Food Code F05040018 is an exact match for wholegrain wheat flour (atta). Values cross-referenced with ICMR-NIN Indian Food Composition Tables 2017 for micronutrient confirmation. All values consistent between sources.

gi_source.title: University of Sydney GI Database — Chapati made from wholegrain atta flour; GI value applied to dry flour as ingredient basis
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 62 is sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database for chapati prepared from wholegrain atta flour (cooked product). The dry flour itself does not have a direct GI measurement; the GI is attributed to the cooked chapati as the primary consumption form. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (62 × 64.8 × 0.30) / 100 ≈ 12.5 for a 30g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: High dietary fibre (11.2g/100g) and magnesium (138mg/100g) with very low sodium (2mg/100g)
hypertension.guidance: Wholegrain atta is suitable for hypertensive patients: its high magnesium content supports arterial relaxation and vascular tone, its dietary fibre contributes to blood pressure reduction, and its negligible sodium content (2mg/100g) poses no concern for sodium-restricted diets.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate GI (62) and high dietary fibre (11.2g/100g) slowing postprandial glucose absorption
type2_diabetes.guidance: Wholegrain atta has a moderate GI of approximately 62 (as chapati), substantially lower than refined wheat flour (maida) or white bread (~70–75); its high dietary fibre content (11.2g/100g) slows gastric emptying and blunts postprandial blood glucose spikes, making it a preferred everyday grain choice for South Asian patients with Type 2 Diabetes who consume flatbreads.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High dietary fibre, low saturated fat (0.4g/100g), zero trans fat, and zero cholesterol
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Wholegrain atta is cardioprotective: its high dietary fibre (11.2g/100g) supports LDL cholesterol reduction, its very low saturated fat (0.4g/100g), zero trans fat, and zero cholesterol content align with cardiovascular risk-reduction dietary guidelines, and its magnesium and potassium content further support heart health.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Magnesium (138mg/100g) and selenium (70.7mcg/100g) supporting immune function and antioxidant defences
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Wholegrain atta provides meaningful amounts of magnesium and selenium, both important for immune regulation and antioxidant defence; it is not considered pro-inflammatory at regular dietary portions and is suitable for patients with rheumatoid arthritis.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Wholegrain wheat flour, commonly known as atta or chakki atta, is flour milled from the entire wheat grain with bran, endosperm, and germ all retained. Stone-ground atta (chakki atta) is produced by grinding wheat between two stone wheels, a process that preserves more of the phytonutrients, bran structure, and natural oils compared to roller-milled wholemeal flour. The result is a light brown, slightly coarse flour with a nutty, earthy aroma and flavour. Atta is finely milled to a soft texture ideal for rolling out unleavened flatbreads such as chapati, roti, and paratha. It is the staple flour of South Asian cooking and is widely sold in Singapore at Indian provision shops and major supermarkets in 5kg bags under brands such as Aashirvaad, Pillsbury, and Pride. Nutritionally, wholegrain atta is significantly superior to refined white flour (maida): it provides 13.3g protein per 100g (including gluten for dough elasticity), 11.2g dietary fibre, 64.8g carbohydrate, and a broad range of B vitamins including thiamin (0.45mg), niacin (5.5mg), and vitamin B6 (0.41mg). Its moderate glycemic index of approximately 62 (as chapati) is lower than white bread, making it a preferred grain for diabetic patients following South Asian dietary patterns. The high magnesium content (138mg/100g) and low sodium (2mg/100g) make it suitable for hypertensive patients. It is naturally vegan, halal, and dairy-free, though it contains gluten and is not suitable for coeliac patients.
common_user_queries: is atta better than maida for diabetes

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo Database (Food Code F05040018), an approved primary regional source, with cross-reference confirmation from ICMR-NIN Indian Food Composition Tables 2017. GI sourced from University of Sydney GI Database for chapati from wholegrain atta. Ontology codes verified against FoodOn, FoodEx2, and SNOMED CT. All values are internally consistent and plausible for wholegrain wheat flour.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for wholegrain atta not separately reported in HPB Foodo Food Code F05040018 or ICMR-NIN tables at this level of granularity. water_g set to -1: moisture content for dry raw atta not reported in the HPB Foodo source record. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero per HPB Foodo source). cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (naturally zero — plant-derived flour contains no cholesterol). vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_d_mcg, and vitamin_b12_mcg are 0.0 (confirmed zero or negligible per HPB Foodo and ICMR-NIN; wholegrain wheat flour does not naturally contain these vitamins in meaningful amounts). GI value of 62 is derived from the University of Sydney GI Database for cooked chapati made from wholegrain atta flour; the dry flour itself does not have a direct GI measurement. Glycemic load per serving calculated for a 30g serving: (62 × 19.44) / 100 ≈ 12.5.
